system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Client Business Intelligence

Systematic approach to researching a client business, identifying opportunities, and preparing a pitch.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Surface-Level Research (30 min)

### Source Priority (run in this order, stop when you have enough)

1. **Their current website** — identify all pages, products, about page, press page, contact. Check for `/nosotros/`, `/tiendas/`, `/prensa/`, `/blog/` — these often have buried gold (subsidiaries, partnerships, director names).
2. **Infonegocios / ABC Color / Última Hora** — PY business press. Use curl + sed for article extraction since these sites are usually accessible without JS. Search: `"{company}" director gerente dueño`
3. **LinkedIn** — `/company/{name}` for corporate page. Note: ALWAYS blocked by auth wall. Can confirm page EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry.
4. **Google Maps** — `https://www.google.com/maps/search/{company}+Paraguay/`. Use the browser tool, then extract `[role=feed]` entries for store names, addresses, ratings, phones.
5. **Facebook** — `/pages/{name}` or `/{name}`. Usually accessible, basic info visible.
6. **DuckDuckGo lite** — `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={query}'` — no-JS, no-block, good for PY corporate queries
7. **Bing** — fallback when Google blocks. `/search?q={query}` with a desktop UA usually works.
8. **Corporate registry** — SET RUC (`dnit.gov.py/web/portal-institucional/consulta-ruc?ruc={RUC}`), Mercantil Registry, InfoCiudad. WARNING: These almost always return 404. Try once then move on.

### Search Queries to Try

```bash
# Ownership/directors
"{company} dueños directores familia"
"{company} gerente director"
"{company} nombre del dueño"

# Subsidiaries
"{company} Argentina filial"
"{company} subsidiaria"

# Press articles
"\"{company}\" Infonegocios"
"\"{company}\" ABC Color"
"\"{company}\" Última Hora"

# General when others fail
curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={company}+Paraguay'
```

**For web scraping without getting blocked:**
- Use `curl -sL 'https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...' -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0'` for search results
- Use `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q=...'` for simple text extraction
- For blocked sites: Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora are good sources for PY corporate news
- LinkedIn auth wall always blocks — note that the profile EXISTS but can't read without login
- Google blocks aggressive IPs — use Bing or DDG as fallback

### Phase 2: Deep Research (1-2 hours)

1. **Read press articles** — search Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora for interviews with directors
2. **Extract key quotes** — director strategy quotes are gold for understanding priorities
3. **Map corporate structure** — parent, subsidiaries, strategic partners, major clients
4. **Product family analysis** — every product line, cross-sell pairs, pricing tiers, warranty differences
5. **Competitive landscape** — list direct competitors, their focus, Superspuma's advantages

### Phase 3: Opportunity Analysis

1. **Cross-sell math** — calculate the upsell potential per customer (e.g., mattress + base + protector + almohada = +Gs. 1.17M)
2. **B2B opportunities** — wholesale client portals, hotel/hospitality programs, government contracts
3. **Market gaps** — what competitors don't do that this client could
4. **Geographic expansion** — Argentina subsidiary (if exists), Brazil border cities, Mercosur advantages

### Phase 4: Contact Documentation

1. **Research the contact** — LinkedIn, news mentions, Google
2. **Document what's known vs unknown** — be honest about gaps
3. **Map authority level** — can they make decisions? Who else needs to be involved?
4. **Build relationship strategy** — warm intro? Cold outreach? Existing relationship?

### Phase 5: Pitch Preparation

Create `contacts/deal-strategy.md` with:
- Deal options (tiered from low to high scope)
- Step-by-step conversation flow (open with value → show → ask → present opportunity → close)
- Talking points (what to highlight, what NOT to say)
- Objection handling for likely concerns
- Competitive positioning (why us vs agencies)
- Pricing signals (monthly retainer, project-based, revenue share)

## File Structure for Client Documentation

```
{client-repo}/
├── contacts/
│   ├── README.md              # Index of all contacts & documents
│   ├── {contact-name}.md       # Profile + relationship strategy
│   └── deal-strategy.md        # Pitch guide + deal options
├── docs/
│   ├── business-analysis.md    # Corporate research
│   └── opportunity-analysis.md # Deal opportunities
```

### Service-at-Home Safety Protocol

When the client offers a **mobile/at-home service** (depilación a domicilio, delivery, etc.), proactively build a safety protocol addressing:

**Risk:** "Alguien pide, voy y me roban."

**Mitigation layers:**
1. **Pre-verification (call, not just text)** — Llmar antes de ir. Si no contesta, no vas.
2. **Location sharing** — Compartir ubicación en vivo con contacto de confianza.
3. **Check-in/out protocol** — "Voy a [dirección]" → "Entré" → "Salí bien". Timer de 30 min.
4. **Intuition rule** — Si algo se siente mal al llegar, cancelás. Decís "perdón, me surgió un problema".
5. **First-session filter** — Ofrecer primera sesión gratis/descuento profundo a las primeras X personas. Sirve como filtro y genera confianza mutua.
6. **No carry cash** — Preferir transferencia bancaria. Menos incentivo al robo.

Documentar en `docs/safety-protocol.md` en el repo del cliente. Incluir checklist diario imprimible y plan de emergencia (qué hacer si pasa algo). Ver `references/service-at-home-safety.md` para template completo.

---

### Phase 6: Service Expansion Research (new service line for existing client)

When a client with an existing business wants to add a NEW service line targeting an underserved segment (e.g., affirmative depilation for trans women, men's grooming, post-op care):

**Step 1: 20-Question Structured Questionnaire with Multiple-Choice Options**

Format: Markdown with 4 blocks of 5 questions each (20 total). Each question has 4-5 pre-generated options (a/b/c/d/e). The MC format forces the client to decide fast instead of rambling.

**Important: include blank "Otro: ________" lines** — clients often combine or modify options. Document their exact words, not the closest option.

Blocks are always:
- Block 1 (Q1-4): Validación personal / Motivación
- Block 2 (Q5-8): Equipo / Técnica
- Block 3 (Q9-12): Precios / Modelo de negocio
- Block 4 (Q13-16): Marketing / Contacto comunitario
- Block 5 (Q17-20): Seguridad / Privacidad / Riesgos

After the client responds, **make the remaining decisions as IA** — don't send the parsed answers back for approval. The client said "decide todo como IA". This includes: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy variations. Document the reasoning for each decision.

If the client leaves a question open ("investiga online y decide"), research and decide. Don't ask again.

2. **Análisis de respuestas** — Las respuestas del dueño revelan prioridades, presupuesto, conocimientos, y miedos. Documentar textualmente, incluyendo correcciones y anotaciones que el dueño agrega a las opciones pre-generadas. Extract all "decide todo como IA" signals and act on them.

3. **Research en 7 lotes paralelos** — Dividir los 100+ temas en lotes: A) Mercado/competencia, B) Máquina/equipo, C) Comunidad, D) Médico, E) Marketing, F) Finanzas, G) Psicología, H) Logística. Investigar cada lote y compilar en un documento master.

4. **Competitor pricing via browser scraping** — Para clínicas/competidores PY:
   - Abrir con `browser_navigate` los sitios de competidores directos
   - Usar `browser_snapshot(full=true)` para capturar precios, zonas, packs
   - Extraer precios reales visibles en el HTML (ej: DepilArte tenía Gs. 49,000-220,000 visibles)
   - Si el sitio está bloqueado, probar `curl -sL` con User-Agent desktop o DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...`)
   - Para sitios sin precios visibles, estimar basado en: tamaño de zona (mini/pink/pequeña/mediana/especial/grande), y precio promedio de mercado PY

5. **Search engine evasion** — Google, Bing, Brave, and Ecosia all block VPS IPs after 1-2 requests. DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`html.duckduckgo.com/html`) works but also blocks after ~3 requests with a CAPTCHA. Recovery strategies:
   - Send requests with rotated User-Agent headers via `curl`
   - Use Browserbase (when available) which routes through residential proxies
   - Fall back to training data knowledge for common business intelligence topics (Paraguay market, IPL pricing, trans community orgs)
   - Accept blocked results as "not accessible via automation" rather than retrying endlessly

6. **Master document compilation** — Después de investigar todos los lotes, compilar en `docs/trans-research-master.md` (o nombre análogo) con:
   - Cada lote como sección A-H
   - Una tabla de resumen ejecutivo al final con decisiones clave (precio, descuento, marketing, plan)
   - Subir a repo y dar link al dueño

7. **100-item research checklist** — After the master document, generate a separate file `docs/{topic}-research-100-list.md` with 100 specific questions/items organized in the same categories. This serves as: progress tracker, idea source for next iterations, and documentation of research scope. Each item is 1 sentence.

8. **Website audit for existing clients** — When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:
   - **Structure map** — every page, component, and content file
   - **Content audit** — es.json (or equivalent): find placeholders ("****"), duplicates (two FAQ sections), missing sections (no mention of new service)
   - **UX/Design** — photos vs emoji, social proof, about section, favicon
   - **SEO** — sitemap, robots, og:image, meta tags, schema
   - **Performance** — DOM size, load time, cache headers
   - **Score each category** 1-10 with justification
   - **Prioritized improvement plan** — 3 tiers: 🔴 esta semana, 🟡 este mes, 🟢 cuando haya tiempo. Each item has: effort estimate, impact assessment, specific files to change.

9. **Action plan from audit** — After the audit:
   - Decide all pricing/package decisions as IA
   - Generate a 7-day checklist (día por día) 
   - Generate a 30-day plan with milestones
   - Generate WhatsApp messages ready to send (4 templates: new client, confirmation, 24h follow-up, 20-day reminder)
   - Push all docs to repo, give client the GitHub link

## Pitfalls

- **LinkedIn always requires login** — accept that you'll only see the auth wall. Note the profile EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry multiple times.
- **Instagram blocks all automated access** — `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE` always. Don't waste time retrying Instagram directly. See `references/instagram-osint.md` for workarounds.
- **Google blocks aggressively** — after 2-3 searches from the same IP, you get a CAPTCHA wall. Switch to DDG lite or Bing immediately.
- **PY corporate registries are unreliable** — SET (DNIT), Mercantil, InfoCiudad often return 404. News articles are more reliable than official registries. Always try once but don't spend more than 30 seconds.
- **Paraguay business owners often have low digital footprint** — many executives have no LinkedIn, no social media. "No public results found" is a valid finding, not a failed search.
- **Warm leads are better than cold research** — if the user has a direct contact (like Leticia Roig as GM of Superspuma), prioritize documenting what they know over searching publicly.
- **Don't rely on browser for everything** — Curl with simple User-Agent headers often works where the headless browser gets blocked. Try `curl -sL` before opening the browser for each source.
- **Infonegocios articles are gold** — they often have director interviews with strategy quotes, company ambitions, competitive claims. Extract the full article text, not just snippets.
- **See `references/search-engine-evasion-pattern.md`** for the full ordered fallback chain when Google/DDG/Bing/Brave all block automated queries. Yahoo is the most reliable VPS-search-engine as of 2026-05. Wikipedia API always works. Instagram data can be extracted from Yahoo search result snippets.

### Pitfall: Template/boilerplate leftovers

When the client site was generated from a multi-tenant builder like `paragu-ai-builder`, check for **stale template files** in `lib/client-kit/` (or equivalent). These are the generic versions of the actual components in `components/`. They are NOT used by the live site but confuse future edits. Recommend cleaning them up in a follow-up commit.

**Detection:** `find . -not -path './node_modules/*' -not -path './.git/*' -not -path './.next/*' -type f | sort` shows component files in TWO locations (e.g. `components/header.tsx` + `lib/client-kit/header.tsx`). The `components/` versions are the live ones.

## Website Audit (for existing clients)

When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:

### Structure map
- Every page, component, and content file. Output: tree of `app/`, `components/`, `content/`.

### Content audit
- Check `es.json` (or equivalent): find **placeholders** (`"****"`, `"placeholder": true`), **duplicates** (two FAQ arrays), **missing sections** (no mention of new service line), **stale references**.
- Check every page — does it load? Does it have content?

### UX/Design scoring
- Score each category 1-10: Funcionalidad, Diseño, Contenido, Prueba Social, SEO, Performance, UX Móvil
- Justify each score in 1 sentence
- Identify the biggest gap (typically "Contenido" or "Prueba Social")

### SEO checklist
- Favicon? (link rel="icon" in head)
- og:image for social shares?
- Sitemap.xml? robots.txt?
- Canonical URL?
- JSON-LD schema?

### Performance
- Load time (from browser performance API)
- DOM size
- Cache headers
- HTTP protocol (h3 is good)

### Prioritized improvement plan
3 tiers with effort estimate and impact:
- 🔴 **Critical** (hacer esta semana) — e.g., favicon, placeholder fix, new service section, Instagram link, photo of owner
- 🟡 **Medium** (hacer este mes) — e.g., real photos, og:image, unified FAQs, social proof counters
- 🟢 **Low** (cuando haya tiempo) — e.g., blog, dark mode, animations, chatbot

Each item should specify the file(s) to change and the exact line/region.

### Implementation after audit

When the client says "implementa todos" or "decide todo como IA":
1. Apply ALL changes identified in the audit
2. For pricing/service decisions left open by the client: research, decide, document reasoning
3. Build: `npm run build` (or equivalent) and fix any errors
4. Git commit + push
5. Docker build + deploy
6. If deploy fails (resources), note it and tell the client what manual step is needed

### Visual Artist Research

When researching a visual artist (muralist, painter, illustrator) for a website project, use `references/artist-visual-research-pattern.md` for the full ordered workflow. Key differences from business research:

- **No corporate registry** — no RUC, no SET. Use Wikidata + Wikipedia API + news sites.
- **Press articles are the richest source** — interviews reveal style, influences, quotes, career timeline.
- **YouTube is underutilized** — extraction of `ytInitialData` from HTML gives video interviews that news articles don't cover.
- **Name confusion is common** — verify with 2+ sources before concluding. Paraguayan artists may have 1-2 press articles but no Wikipedia presence.
- **Website plan is the deliverable** — unlike business research (deal strategy), artist research produces a complete website architecture (10 pages minimum) with content specification per page, tech stack, monetization strategy, and roadmap.

## Per-session reference file management

After each major research session for a client:
1. Create `references/{client}-{topic}-{date}.md` with condensed findings
2. This file is the **session transcript summary** — not a substitute for the full docs in the client repo
3. Keep it focused on: key findings, techniques learned, decisions that future sessions should know
4. Delete the oldest reference when the list gets too long (5+ files) — the repo docs are the source of truth

## `decide-todo-como-IA` pattern

When the client responds to the 20-question questionnaire and leaves decisions open with "decide todo como IA" or "investiga online y decide":
- Research each open question (competitor pricing, technical specs, community contacts)
- Take a firm decision — don't propose options back
- Document the reasoning for each decision in markdown
- Include a table: "Decisiones de la IA basadas en investigación"
- This applies to: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy

## Verification

After creating the documentation files:
1. Push to repo: `git add contacts/ docs/ && git commit && git push`
2. Verify the site content reflects the research (update es.json with new company info)
3. If adding stores with GPS, add Google Maps links to the store locator page
4. Cross-sell opportunities should be reflected in product page design (show bundles)

name: brand-positioning-from-repos
description: Extract brand positioning, audience definition, customer pains, and landing page copy from a company's actual project repos — not theory. Use when a founder says "help me with positioning" or needs website copy grounded in reality.
version: 1.0.0

    tags: [positioning, branding, copywriting, landing-page, audience, strategy]

# Brand Positioning from Repo Analysis

Derive positioning, audience, pains, and conversion copy from what a company has actually built — not aspirational claims.

## When to Use

- Founder asks for help with positioning, messaging, or landing page copy
- Company website doesn't match what the company actually does
- Need to tighten audience definition with evidence
- Writing or rewriting a homepage/landing page

## Step 1: Repo Audit for Audience Extraction

Pull all repos with metadata:
```bash
gh repo list ORG --limit 50 --json name,description,primaryLanguage,repositoryTopics,isPrivate,pushedAt | cat
```

Categorize every repo by CLIENT TYPE (not tech stack):
- Healthcare, Retail, Energy, Automotive, Education, etc.
- Note which are client projects vs internal tools vs research

### Key Questions to Answer
- Who is ACTUALLY paying? (client projects reveal real audience)
- What industries repeat? (pattern = sweet spot)
- What's the company size of clients? (SMB vs enterprise)
- What geography? (but don't limit to geography unless founder wants to)

## Step 2: Positioning Sentence

Format: "We help [specific audience] achieve [specific outcome] using [unique mechanism]"

### Rules
- Audience = defined by SITUATION not demographics or geography
- Outcome = what the client gets, not what you do
- Mechanism = what makes you different from alternatives
- Never mention tool names (no "using Claude and Cursor")
- Provide 3-4 options, recommend one, let founder choose direction

### Common Trap
Founder says "our audience is anyone who needs X" — repos always tell a tighter story. Present the evidence, let them decide whether to broaden.

## Step 3: Pain Extraction

Derive 3-5 pains from the TYPE of projects built:
- If clients had no tech team → "We don't have developers and can't afford to hire"
- If projects replaced manual workflows → "Everything is still manual"
- If clients tried generic tools first → "We tried ChatGPT but couldn't make it work for our business"
- If projects were rebuilds/rescues → "We paid for software before and it never got finished"
- If training was part of delivery → "We don't know where to start with AI"

Each pain must map to something the company actually solved in a real project.

## Step 4: Current Website Gap Analysis

Extract current site content and compare against repo reality:
```
web_extract(["https://company-site.com"])
```

Check for:
1. Hero talks about tools instead of outcomes → rewrite
2. No problem/pain section → visitor has no "that's me" moment
3. Testimonials look fake (generic names, no photos) → hurts more than helps, remove or replace with real case studies
4. Geography limiting ("serving X region") when skills are market-agnostic → drop unless strategic
5. Mixed languages in copy → looks unfinished
6. Internal goals stated as client benefits ("our vision is to be the leader in X") → remove
7. Generic service descriptions that match every competitor → differentiate

## Step 5: Landing Page Content Template

Fill this structure — every section pushes toward CTA:

### 1. HERO (above the fold)
- Headline: Main outcome in simple terms
- Subheadline: Who you help + problem + what you do differently
- CTA: Primary action (Book a call)
- Trust line: Quick credibility from real projects

### 2. PROBLEM (recognition)
- "Does this sound familiar?"
- 3-5 pain bullets from Step 3

### 3. SOLUTION (your approach)
- Reframing idea (what you do differently)
- 2-3 line explanation
- 3 key differentiators

### 4. OUTCOMES (results)
- 4-6 concrete results clients get
- Use language from actual project deliverables

### 5. TRUST (proof)
- Mini cases from REAL repos (anonymize if needed but keep industry)
- Industries served (extracted from repo categories)
- Only use testimonials if they're real

### 6. PROCESS (reduce complexity)
- 3-4 steps max
- Understand → Design → Build → Deliver+Train

### 7. FINAL CTA
- Reinforce outcome
- Remove fear/friction
- Same CTA as hero

## Rules
- No paragraph longer than 2-3 lines
- No jargon
- If it doesn't help conversion → remove it
- Every claim must trace back to a real project
- Don't use fake testimonial names — use "Verified Client" or real ones

## Pitfalls

1. **Don't let founder's aspiration override evidence** — "we serve everyone" is never true. Show the repo data.
2. **Geography is where you've been, not where you have to stay** — unless founder wants geographic focus, define audience by situation not location.
3. **Tool names in hero copy kill conversion** — nobody outside tech knows what Cursor or [REMOVED] is.
4. **Fake testimonials hurt more than no testimonials** — generic names + generic titles + no photos = instant credibility loss.
5. **Internal vision ≠ client benefit** — "position Paraguay as AI leader" means nothing to a prospect.

for r in repos:
    lang = r['primaryLanguage']['name'] if r['primaryLanguage'] else 'N/A'
    topics = r.get('repositoryTopics') or []
    desc = r['description'] or '(no description)'
    print(f\"{r['name']:35s} | {lang:12s} | {desc:.70s}\")
" | cat", "timeout": 15}

{"code": "from hermes_tools import read_file, write_file

content = read_file("/tmp/nico-portfolio/app/page.tsx")["content"]

# Replace all old purple references with new deeper mystic
replacements = [
    ("rgba(139,92,246,0.4)", "rgba(142,26,255,0.4)"),
    ("bg-mystic-950/60", "bg-mystic-950/60"),
    ("border-mystic-700/50", "border-mystic-700/50"),
    ("text-mystic-300", "text-mystic-300"),
    ("text-mystic-400", "text-mystic-400"),
    ("text-mystic-500", "text-mystic-500"),
    ("border-mystic-500/50", "border-mystic-500/50"),
    ("bg-mystic-900/30", "bg-mystic-900/30"),
    ("from-purple-500 to-violet-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-700"),
    ("from-violet-500 to-indigo-600", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-800"),
    ("from-fuchsia-500 to-purple-600", "from-mystic-400 to-mystic-700"),
    ("from-purple-500 to-pink-600", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-600"),
    ("from-indigo-500 to-purple-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-500"),
    ("from-violet-500 to-fuchsia-600", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-400"),
    ("from-purple-600 to-violet-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-500"),
    ("from-purple-500 to-violet-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-700"),
    ("bg-mystic-800/50", "bg-mystic-800/50"),
    ("border-mystic-700/40", "border-mystic-700/40"),
    ("bg-mystic-500", "bg-mystic-500"),
    ("from-mystic-500 to-mystic-400", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-400"),
    ("text-mystic-500", "bg-mystic-500"),
    ("text-mystic-500", "text-mystic-500"),
    ("border-mystic-800/30", "border-mystic-800/30"),
]

# Remove redundant from-purple-600 that doesn't match
# Let me just check what patterns exist
import re
purple_refs = re.findall(r'(from-[\w-]+|to-[\w-]+|bg-[\w/-]+|text-[\w-]+|border-[\w/-]+|rgba\([^)]+\))', content)
unique = set(purple_refs)
print("Found color refs:", sorted(unique))

# Check for remaining old purple after our replacements
old_purple = [r for r in unique if 'purple' in r or 'violet' in r or 'fuchsia' in r or 'indigo' in r or 'pink' in r]
print("\nOld tailwind colors remaining:", old_purple)
"}

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "client-content-audit", "description": "Audit a client repo's content against a questionnaire/source-of-truth — find gaps, fix them, and surface what the client still needs to provide. Covers questionnaire ingestion, gap analysis, source-of-truth generation, and content implementation.", "tags": ["client", "content", "audit", "questionnaire", "gap-analysis", "paraguai", "dayah", "el-viajero"], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: client-content-audit
description: "Audit a client repo's content against a questionnaire/source-of-truth — find gaps, fix them, and surface what the client still needs to provide. Covers questionnaire ingestion, gap analysis, source-of-truth generation, and content implementation."
tags: [client, content, audit, questionnaire, gap-analysis, paraguai, dayah, el-viajero]
---

# Client Content Audit & Questionnaire Implementation

Audit a client website repo against their filled questionnaire / source-of-truth. Find every gap, fix what can be fixed with data already available, and document what requires client action.

## When to Use

- Client provided a questionnaire with answers and you need to verify implementation
- Taking over a partially-built client site and need to assess completeness
- Before client relaunch — ensure all content promises are delivered
- After a conversation with the client produced new data that needs to be incorporated

## Process

### 1. Ingest the Questionnaire

The questionnaire may be in `docs/` as `questionnaire.md`, `source-of-truth.md`, or `client-questionnaire.md`. Three tiers of detail:

- **`client-questionnaire.md`** — blank template (2KB, section headings only, no answers)
- **`questionnaire.md`** — filled form with client's answers (15-20KB)
- **`source-of-truth.md`** — structured canonical reference (10-15KB)

Read all three. The `source-of-truth.md` is the most authoritative — if it exists, use it as primary.

### 2. Map Questionnaire -> Content Inventory

Check every section of the questionnaire against the app's content:

```python
import json

# Read content
content = json.load(open("content/es.json"))
content_str = json.dumps(content).lower()

# Check each field
gaps = []
if "5618487" not in content_str:
    gaps.append("RUC not in content")
```

### 3. Categorize Gaps

Use a structured gap analysis with emoji prefix:
- `🔴 Critical` — content is missing and page will 404 or error (e.g., privacidad/terminos content sections don't exist but pages reference them)
- `🟡 Medium` — content exists in questionnaire but not on site (testimonials, services, social links)
- `🟢 Minor` — nice-to-have info (business hours, city details)

### 4. Fix What Can Be Fixed NOW

**Testimonials:** Client may have provided 5+ real testimonials with permission. Replace the placeholder/generic ones. Include: name, country, service received, quote, rating. DO NOT fabricate names — use verified client names only.

**Social Links:** The client's questionnaire almost always has Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, Wattpad, Amazon Author links that are nowhere on the site. Add them to the footer's `social` array and to the contact section.

**Missing Services:** The questionnaire may list 12+ services with prices, but the site may only show 8. Add the missing ones. Each needs: id, name, description, priceUSD, pricePYG, delivery time.

**Education/Credentials:** Client certifications (MIT, Google, etc.) and education rarely make it onto the about page. Add them even if the site already has a bio — this is high-CL (credibility load) content.

**RUC/Legal Name:** In Paraguay, displaying the RUC number on the footer is standard for professional services. Add it if missing.

**Work Process:** If the client provided a detailed work process (6+ steps with durations), add it to the services page as a visible process section.

**Blog:** If content has blog posts (`blog.posts`) but no `/blog` route exists, create the pages. Posts often have slug, title, excerpt, date, category, author, readingTime.

### 5. Sync to English (or secondary locale)

Most LATAM client sites are bilingual (ES/EN). After fixing ES content, sync the same changes to EN. Watch out for different key structures — ES may use `about.story.paragraphs` while EN uses `sobre.bio.features`. Check keys before blind-copying.

### 6. Generate/Save source-of-truth.md

If the client's filled questionnaire data is spread across conversation context and not saved to the repo, write it as `docs/source-of-truth.md`. This file becomes THE canonical reference for all future work. Structure it as:

```markdown
# Client Name — Source of Truth

> Complete client questionnaire answers. Canonical reference for all content.
> Source: Client onboarding session — Month Year

## PART A — CONFIRMATIONS

### A.1 Brand Identity
| Field | Confirmed Value | Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Business name | ✅ | ✅ |
| RUC | [REDACTED_PHONE] | ❌ NOT IN CONTENT |

### A.3 Services with Prices ✅
| # | Service | USD | PYG | Delivery | Implemented |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Custom Cover eBook | $45 | ₲300,000 | 1-2 weeks | ✅ |
```

### 7. What to Leave for Client Action

Some things cannot be fixed by the agent and must be surfaced as "Client Action Still Needed":
- **Image files** (premade catalog, professional photos, portfolio samples) — client must upload to Drive
- **Final brand palette** (exact hex codes, typography) — pending client design decisions
- **Mercado Pago/PayPal confirmation** — client must verify account
- **Domain registration** — client must purchase and point DNS

## Reference Implementation

**Full Dayah LitWorks audit documented in session:**
- Questionaire ingested: 20KB+ with 12 sections
- 10 gaps found and fixed
- `docs/source-of-truth.md` generated (14KB)
- `docs/client-profile.md` — client achievement audit
- All changes synced ES/EN and pushed

## Pitfalls

- **Git disagreements** — If the repo has diverged branches or force-push history, `git reset --hard origin/main` may wipe your changes. Always `git pull --rebase` first, or `git stash` before resetting.
- **EN vs ES key structures differ** — Never assume keys match. `content_es` might have `about` while `content_en` has `sobre`. Check both before syncing.
- **Content size limits** — `content/es.json` can reach 50KB+ with blog posts and full FAQ. Keep the JSON valid with `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False)` for Spanish characters.
- **Client provided testimonials but no explicit permission** — The questionnaire usually states permission status. Trust data from filled questionnaires. If unsure, use "Verified Client" instead of fabricated names.

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

Good session. Let me check what skills were in play and what we learned.

**Signal analysis:**
- User called the site "ugly" — that's a UX/design quality signal, not a workflow correction

- The redesign work was for Nico's portfolio specifically, not a general technique

The Porto design overhaul isn't a new general skill — it's what `brand-positioning-from-repos` would produce after repo analysis. The deploy workflow is already covered by existing Docker/Traefik skills.

# VPS Hardening — Docker Swarm + Compose Hybrid

## When to Use
- Setting up or hardening a single-node Docker Swarm VPS
- Adding new services behind Traefik reverse proxy
- After Docker restart/upgrade (services need reconnection)
- Setting up automated backups for Docker infrastructure

## Phase 1: Security Hardening

### 1a. UFW Firewall
CRITICAL: Allow SSH FIRST before enabling, or you get locked out.

```bash
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw allow 22/tcp comment 'SSH'
ufw allow 80/tcp comment 'HTTP'
ufw allow 443/tcp comment 'HTTPS'
# Docker internal ranges
ufw allow from 172.16.0.0/12 comment 'Docker internal'
ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/8 comment 'Docker overlay'
ufw allow from 192.168.0.0/16 comment 'Docker bridge'
# Swarm ports
ufw allow 2377/tcp comment 'Swarm management'
ufw allow 7946/tcp comment 'Swarm node comm'
ufw allow 7946/udp comment 'Swarm node comm'
ufw allow 4789/udp comment 'Swarm overlay'
echo 'y' | ufw enable
```

This automatically blocks any services on non-standard ports (like Qdrant 6333, LiteLLM 4000) from external access while keeping them reachable via Docker networks.

### 1b. Swap Space
Even with 32GB RAM, add swap as an OOM safety net:
```bash
fallocate -l 4G /swapfile
chmod 600 /swapfile
mkswap /swapfile
swapon /swapfile
echo '/swapfile none swap sw 0 0' >> /etc/fstab
sysctl vm.swappiness=10
echo 'vm.swappiness=10' >> /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf
```

### 1c. Docker daemon.json

**PITFALL: `live-restore: true` is INCOMPATIBLE with Docker Swarm mode.**
Docker will fail to start with: `failed to start cluster component: --live-restore daemon configuration is incompatible with swarm mode`

Correct config for Swarm:
```json
{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "default-ulimits": {
    "nofile": {"Name": "nofile", "Hard": 65536, "Soft": 65536}
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2",
  "metrics-addr": "127.0.0.1:9323"
}
```

After writing to `/etc/docker/daemon.json`:
```bash
systemctl restart docker
```

**PITFALL: Docker restart on Swarm loses locally-built images.**
Swarm services using local images (built with `docker build`, not pulled from registry) will go to 0/1 replicas with "No such image" error. Must rebuild them after restart:
```bash
cd /path/to/project && docker build -t image-name:latest .
# Swarm will automatically pick up the new image
```

## Phase 2: Traefik + Swarm/Compose Hybrid Networking

### The Core Problem
Traefik runs as a Swarm service on an overlay network (`agent-net`). Compose projects CANNOT attach to Swarm overlay networks (`rpc error: network agent-net not manually attachable`).

### Solution: Bridge Networks + Traefik Reconnection
Each Compose project uses its own bridge network. Traefik connects to each bridge network to discover and route to them.

Compose project pattern:
```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    image: myapp:latest
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.docker.network=myapp-net"   # CRITICAL - see below
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.rule=Host(`myapp.example.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.tls=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
      - "traefik.http.services.myapp.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
    networks:
      - myapp-net

networks:
  myapp-net:
    name: myapp-net
```

**CRITICAL: `traefik.docker.network` label is REQUIRED** when Traefik has `--providers.docker.network=agent-net` (Swarm default). Without it, Traefik tries to route through agent-net overlay and gets 404/502. The label tells Traefik which bridge network to use to reach the Compose container. This was the root cause of new services returning 404 — Traefik discovered them but couldn't route to them.

**PITFALL: Labels must be at service level (not under `deploy:`) for Compose projects.** Swarm services use `deploy.labels`, Compose services use service-level `labels`.

**PITFALL: `docker service update --label-add` does NOT persist labels to `TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec`** where Traefik's Docker Swarm provider reads from. The labels go to a different field and Traefik ignores them. Always use `docker stack deploy -c compose.yml` with labels in the compose file.

### Traefik Reconnection Script
After Docker restart, Traefik loses bridge network connections. Create `/opt/scripts/traefik-reconnect.sh`:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
TRAEFIK_ID=$(docker ps -q -f name=traefik_traefik)
[ -z "$TRAEFIK_ID" ] && echo "Traefik not running!" && exit 1

# Re-apply postgres alias on pg-bridge (lost on container restart)
PG_ID=$(docker ps -q -f name=postgres_postgres)
[ -n "$PG_ID" ] && docker network connect --alias postgres pg-bridge "$PG_ID" 2>/dev/null || true

NETWORKS=(myapp-net another-net)  # list all bridge networks
for net in "${NETWORKS[@]}"; do
    docker network connect "$net" "$TRAEFIK_ID" 2>/dev/null && \
        echo "Connected: $net" || echo "Already: $net"
done
```

Automate with cron and systemd:
```bash
# Cron @reboot
@reboot sleep 30 && /opt/scripts/traefik-reconnect.sh >> /opt/backups/traefik-reconnect.log 2>&1

# Docker post-start hook: /etc/systemd/system/docker.service.d/traefik-reconnect.conf
[Service]
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c 'sleep 15 && /opt/scripts/traefik-reconnect.sh &'
```

### Compose→Swarm PostgreSQL Access
Compose containers can't reach Swarm services directly. Solution: bridge network with alias.

```bash
docker network create pg-bridge
# Connect Swarm postgres with a stable alias
docker network connect --alias postgres pg-bridge $(docker ps -q -f name=postgres_postgres)
# Connect Compose services
docker network connect pg-bridge myapp
```

In Compose files, add pg-bridge as external network:
```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    networks:
      - myapp-net
      - pg-bridge
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: [REDACTED_DB_URL]  myapp-net:
    name: myapp-net
  pg-bridge:
    external: true
```

**PITFALL: The postgres alias on pg-bridge must be re-applied after Docker restart** (Swarm container gets new ID). Add to reconnect script.

## Phase 3: Automated Backups

Daily backup script (`/opt/backups/backup.sh`):
1. `pg_dumpall` from postgres container → gzipped
2. Docker volume backup via alpine tar container
3. Config files (stack yamls, .env, Hermes config)
4. Retention: 7 days

Cron schedule:
```
0 3 * * * /opt/backups/backup.sh          # PG + volumes + configs
0 4 * * * /opt/backups/qdrant-backup.sh   # Qdrant snapshots
0 5 * * 0 docker system prune -f --filter "until=72h"  # Weekly cleanup
```

## Phase 4: Monitoring Stack (Prometheus + Grafana)

Deploy a monitoring stack for host + Docker metrics:

```yaml
# /opt/monitoring/docker-compose.yml
services:
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:latest
    volumes:
      - ./prometheus/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
      - prometheus_data:/prometheus
    ports:
      - "9090:9090"
    command:
      - "--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml"

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana:latest
    volumes:
      - grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana
      - ./grafana/provisioning:/etc/grafana/provisioning
    ports:
      - "3001:3000"  # host 3001 to avoid conflicts
    environment:
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD: [REDACTED]  prometheus_data:
  grafana_data:
```

Enable Docker metrics in `/etc/docker/daemon.json`:
```json
{
  "metrics-addr": "0.0.0.0:9323",
  "experimental": true
}
```

Node exporter (Swarm global service):
```bash
docker service create --mode global --name node-exporter \
  --publish mode=host,target=9100 \
  prom/node-exporter:latest
```

Prometheus config (`/opt/monitoring/prometheus/prometheus.yml`):
```yaml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  - job_name: "node"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["node-exporter:9100"]
  - job_name: "docker"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["host.docker.internal:9323"]
```

**PITFALL: Grafana datasource config** — only one datasource can be `isDefault: true`. If you get "Datasource provisioning error", remove `isDefault: true` from your datasource YAML.

**PITFALL: Port conflicts** — check what's on 3000 first with `ss -tlnp | grep :3000`. Use 3001+ for Grafana if taken.

**PITFALL: Compose `deploy:` block** — in `docker compose` (v2), the `deploy` block is ignored. Use simple `ports:` and `volumes:` instead.

Traefik route for Grafana (`/opt/traefik/dynamic/grafana.yml`):
```yaml
http:
  routers:
    grafana:
      rule: "Host(`metrics.example.com`)"
      entrypoints:
        - websecure
      service: grafana
      tls:
        certresolver: letsencrypt
  services:
    grafana:
      loadBalancer:
        servers:
          - url: "http://127.0.0.1:3001"
```

Recommended additions with Traefik routing:
- **Uptime Kuma** (~100MB) — status.domain.com — monitoring + alerts
- **n8n** (~230MB) — n8n.domain.com — AI workflow automation
- **Open WebUI** (~400MB) — chat.domain.com — LLM chat interface
- **Umami** (~100MB) — analytics.domain.com — privacy analytics

All need DNS A records pointing to the VPS IP.

## Pitfalls Summary

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Docker won't start after daemon.json | `live-restore: true` + Swarm | Remove `live-restore` from daemon.json |
| Compose can't use Swarm overlay | Overlay networks not attachable | Use bridge networks + Traefik reconnect |
| Swarm service 0/1 after restart | Locally-built image lost | Rebuild: `docker build -t name:latest .` |
| New Compose service unreachable | Traefik not on its network | `docker network connect net traefik-id` |
| Compose can't reach Swarm DB | Different network namespace | pg-bridge with `--alias postgres` |
| Labels ignored in Compose | Labels under `deploy:` | Move labels to service level |
| Umami/n8n can't auth to postgres | User/DB not created | Create DB+user in postgres BEFORE starting |
| Docker prune removes active images | Swarm images are "unused" during restart | Prune AFTER all services are stable |
| fun4me-style: stale network label | Old network exists with wrong labels | `docker network rm` then recreate |
| Stack deploy ignores build: in compose | Must build image separately first | `docker build ...` THEN `docker stack deploy` |
| Stale Traefik router after stack rename | Old service router lingers | `docker stack rm` old stack fully before redeploying |
| **Next.js standalone container returns 504/000** | Server listening on container hostname, not `0.0.0.0` | Add `ENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` to Dockerfile after `ENV PORT=3000` |
| Cf API token has no DNS write | Token scope = zone:read only | Add DNS A records manually in Cloudflare dashboard |
| LE rate limited on new subdomain | DNS didn't exist during first LE attempt | Wait 1 hour, DNS must resolve before Traefik sees it |
| Port 3000 conflict on restart | Multiple services bind same host port | Remove `ports:` from Compose — let Traefik handle routing |
| 404 with Traefik + Compose | Missing `traefik.docker.network` label | Add label pointing to the service's bridge network |
| pg-bridge alias lost | Swarm PG container restarted (new ID) | Re-run `docker network connect --alias postgres pg-bridge` |
| UFW locks you out | Enabled before allowing SSH | ALWAYS `ufw allow 22/tcp` first |
| **Shell broken after deleting CWD** | Ran `rm -rf` on dir the shell was `cd`'d into | Use `background=true` flag for destructive ops on the shell's CWD; or `cd /` first |
| **`docker service update --force` appears to work but old code still runs** | Image digest unchanged — same tag (`latest`) pointed at same digest from previous build | Add a no-op layer to force new digest: `RUN echo $(date +%s) > /build-id`. Or use unique tags per build. **Verify** with: `docker exec $(docker ps -f name=service -q | head -1) grep -c 'NewFeatureName' /app/.next/static/chunks/app/page-*.js` |
| **nginx listening on 8080, never receiving traffic** | Cloudflare origin → VPS:443 → Traefik, bypassing nginx entirely | Check `ss -tlnp` for ghost nginx on non-standard ports; if not in any CF origin rule, it's dead weight |
| **Swarm service has no Traefik labels** | Service deployed without compose file | Create compose with Traefik labels → `docker stack deploy -c` |
| **Site returns 000 from curl** | DNS does not resolve — no A record in Cloudflare | Add `domain.com A VPS_IP` in Cloudflare DNS dashboard |
| **LE fails: DNS not propagated** | Just-added A record not resolved globally | Wait up to 1 hour; Traefik retries every ~15s |
| **File write didn't persist** | `cat >` heredoc in a piped/batched shell command | Use `write_file` tool for critical files |
| **fun4me: 136MB node_modules in git history** | node_modules or `.next` was committed | `git checkout --orphan clean-main` to create fresh history, then force-push |
| **Alejandro repo: empty, no DNS, no content** | Stale scaffold with no actual site | Delete Docker service + GH repo. Tokens with `admin:org` but no `delete_repo` scope need manual deletion. |
| **Clinica: old container on bridge, not in Traefik** | Was deployed via `docker compose` not `docker stack` | Stop old container, copy app into monorepo `apps/`, standardize Dockerfile/compose/Traefik labels, deploy via stack |
| **@ai-whisperers packages not resolvable during Docker build** | `.npmrc` with GH token not in build context | For now, don't add @ai-whisperers deps until packages are public or Docker build can mount the token |
| **Site returns 404 from standalone Next.js catch-all** | `[...slug]` route needs a slug; root `/` is not handled | Access via PAB path or add a root handler in the app |
| **Traefik rule with accented chars (ü, ñ, é) never matches** | Browsers/punycode middleboxes send Host as `xn--ndo-hoa.paragu-ai.com`, not `nüdo.paragu-ai.com` | Use punycode form in Traefik rule: `Host('xn--ndo-hoa.paragu-ai.com')`. Find with: `python3 -c \"print('nüdo.com'.encode('idna').decode())\"` |
| **Next.js standalone server 504/000 on public URL** | Server listening on container hostname, not `0.0.0.0` | Add `ENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` to Dockerfile after `ENV PORT=3000` |
| **New Swarm service unreachable (000 curl)** | Missing `traefik.docker.network=agent-net` deploy label | Add label under `deploy.labels` in docker-compose.yml |
| **Hero CSS background-image 404** | CSS refs `/images/hero-bg.svg` but file was never created | Remove the CSS ref from hero.tsx or add the SVG to public/images/ |
| **Generic template copy shared across all sites** | Auto-generator used same tagline/icons for gym, spa, salon | Per-site services.tsx with unique content, icons (Dumbbell, Flower2, Scissors instead of all Scissors) |
| **Site returns 526 (Invalid SSL cert) from Cloudflare** | New proxied subdomain — CF defaults to Full (Strict) mode, but origin has self-signed cert | **Sequence:** (1) Create self-signed cert on Traefik via file provider; (2) Change CF SSL to Full (not Strict) in dashboard. Cannot use LE ACME (CF proxy blocks HTTP-01) or CF Origin CA API (token lacks write). See `references/cloudflare-526-ssl-fix.md` for full sequence and pitfalls. |
| **nginx listening on 8080, never receiving traffic** | Cloudflare origin → VPS:443 → Traefik, bypassing nginx:8080 entirely | Check `ss -tlnp` for ghost nginx on non-standard ports; if not in any CF origin rule, it's dead weight |
| **Swarm Docker service has no Traefik labels** | Service deployed without compose file | `[REDACTED_DOCKER_CMD]` shows X/X replicas but `docker service inspect` has empty labels | Create a compose file with Traefik labels and `docker stack deploy -c` |
| **Site returns 000 from curl** | DNS does not resolve — no A record in Cloudflare | Add `domain.com A VPS_IP` in Cloudflare DNS dashboard; services running but unreachable |
| **LE fails: DNS not propagated** | Just-added A record not resolved globally | Wait up to 1 hour; Traefik retries every ~15s |

## Appendix: GitHub Packages Publishing on this VPS

### Key constraint: scope must match org name
When publishing to GitHub Packages, the npm scope MUST match the GitHub org name exactly (case-insensitive lookup). For `Ai-Whisperers` org, the scope is `@ai-whisperers`.

```bash
# Setup .npmrc
echo "//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=$(gh auth token)" > .npmrc
echo "@ai-whisperers:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com/" >> .npmrc

# Publish (--userconfig required to read the file)
npm publish --userconfig .npmrc

# Token must have write:packages scope
# Check: gh auth status --show-token | grep write:packages
```

### Common failures
- **401 "unauthenticated"** — `.npmrc` not found or `_authToken` env var not expanded. Write inline token, not `$GH_TOKEN`.
- **403 "owner not found"** — Scope doesn't match org name. `@paragu-ai` fails on `Ai-Whisperers` org. Use `@ai-whisperers`.
- **403 "forbidden by security policy"** — Token has `repo` and `write:packages` but scope is wrong.
- **409 "Cannot publish over existing version"** — Bump version in `package.json` and retry.

## Appendix: Hermes Cron Job Management on Swarm VPS

When managing 23+ cron jobs on a VPS running 6+ Docker services, use this audit pattern:

### Cron Job Health Check
```bash
hermes cron list
```
Check `last_status` and `last_delivery_error` for every job. Common failures:
- **`deliver: "whatsapp"` without target ID** — errors with "no delivery target resolved". Fix: set `deliver: "local"` or `deliver: "whatsapp:username"`.
- **`model: null` / `provider: null`** — inherits current session model (cheap). Fix: explicitly set `model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` and `provider: "openrouter"`.
- **Missing `enabled_toolsets`** — skill has tools but job can't use them. Fix: add `["terminal", "file", "skills"]`.

### Automated Worker Pattern (1000-item backlog)
For large autonomous build backlogs, use self-scheduling cron jobs:
1. Create a job that runs every 20m
2. Each execution picks ONE item from a markdown backlog, implements it, marks it `[✅]`
3. Verifies the result with curl/http check
4. Self-schedules continuation (the job's `repeat: forever` handles this — no explicit rescheduling needed)
5. The backlog IS the state — no database, no progress tracking

**Key constraints for autonomous workers:**
- NEVER run without explicit `model` + `provider` (inherits cheap model)
- NEVER use `deliver: "whatsapp"` without a specific target
- Always include `enabled_toolsets` matching the required skill
- Use `skill` parameter to load the correct skill module
- Workers need `terminal` + `file` at minimum; `skills` if they load a skill

### Jobs to Remove
Any job that:
- Never ran (`last_run_at: null`)
- Errors consistently with no fix path
- Was created as a test/experiment with no real purpose
- Has no model/provider set

### Deliver Targets Audit
Correct targets for this VPS:
- `local` — saves to log, no external delivery
- `whatsapp:weissvanderpol` — DM to user (lead-scout only)
- `telegram:Fun4me-IA` — group delivery

NEVER use bare `whatsapp` or bare `telegram` — they error.

See also: `references/traefik-swarm-tenant-pattern.md` — pure Swarm tenant deployment patterns (no Compose bridge hacks), DNS setup, and Swarm-specific deploy pitfalls.
See also: `references/standalone-tenant-investigation.md` — how to find and inspect standalone tenant sites deployed as Docker Swarm services from independent repos (Ai-Whisperers org, not the monorepo). Covers content layer inspection, image placeholder patterns, and key differences from monorepo-based tenants.
See also: `references/repo-cleanup-pattern.md` — batch /tmp/ cleanup, GitHub archival (gh api -X PATCH -f archived=true), cross-org duplicate detection (Ai-Whisperers vs IvanWeissVanDerPol), and the delete_repo scope limitation.
See also: `references/monorepo-scaffold-pattern.md` — pnpm monorepo package template, tools pattern, and file persistence notes (use write_file tool for critical files, not cat heredocs).
See also: `references/nginx-pab-multitenant-monorepo.md` — nginx → PAB proxy architecture (paragu-ai.com is nginx, not Traefik), monorepo package inventory (github.com/Ai-Whisperers/paragu-ai with 9 shared packages), Dockerfile migration to base image, and decision matrix for brochure vs standalone vs e-commerce sites.
See also: `references/github-packages-publish-pattern.md` — complete GitHub Packages publish flow: scope must match org name, .npmrc setup, common 401/403/409 errors, and token requirements.
See also: `references/multi-repo-mass-update-pattern.md` — batch Dockerfile + .npmrc + package migration across 8 repos, orphaned git branches after `git checkout --orphan`, and force-push workaround when git history has large binary files.
See also: `references/site-roast-audit-pattern.md` — full-site audit workflow: inventory, browser scan, performance check, roast delivery format with cross-site analysis checklist and ranking formula.
See also: `references/repo-lifecycle-migration-pattern.md` — stop old static container → archive old repo → copy into monorepo apps/ dir → standardize Dockerfile/compose/Traefik → stack deploy from monorepo. Used for clinica-duerksen. Domain change pattern: `duerksen.sunstein.cloud` → `clinica.paragu-ai.com` (consistent with other sites).

```bash
# System
free -h                    # RAM + swap
ufw status                 # firewall rules
cat /etc/docker/daemon.json # log rotation

# Docker
docker ps --format '{{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}' | sort
[REDACTED_DOCKER_CMD]          # Swarm services (all should show X/X)
docker stats --no-stream   # resource usage per container
docker system df           # disk usage

# Backups
ls -lh /opt/backups/pg/    # recent dumps
crontab -l                 # backup schedules

# Networking
docker network ls          # all networks
# For each compose service, verify Traefik can see it:
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://service.domain.com
```

## Quick Diagnostic: "Site running but returning 000"

When `curl -so /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://domain.com` returns `000`:

1. **DNS check**: `dig +short domain.com` — empty means no A record in Cloudflare
2. **Docker check**: `[REDACTED_DOCKER_CMD] | grep service_name` — X/X replicas means it's running
3. **Traefik route check**: `docker service inspect svc_name --format '{{range $k,$v := .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}{{$k}}={{$v}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}' | grep traefik` — empty labels = no route
4. **Fix DNS**: Add A record manually in Cloudflare dashboard (CF token is read-only)
5. **Fix labels**: Create compose file with labels → `docker stack deploy -c compose.yml`

name: professional-portfolio
description: Scaffold a single-page portfolio website from someone's professional profile, resume, or project data. Next.js 15 + Tailwind CSS 4 static export. Custom theme per user request.
tags: [portfolio, nextjs, tailwindcss, static-site, personal-branding, web-development]

  - portfolio website for [name]

---

# Professional Portfolio Site

Scaffold a production-ready single-page portfolio for a professional (analyst, developer, designer, etc.) using Next.js 15 + Tailwind CSS 4 with a custom color theme.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Gather Profile Data

Before building, gather or confirm:
- **Name** and profession/title
- **Employer** and role (past + present)
- **Key projects** (3-6 items): title, description, technologies, metrics
- **Skills** (8-12 items with proficiency %)
- **Contact info** (what channels)
- **Color theme preference** (exact color name or reference)

When available from prior session context (via session_search), extract and adapt.

### Phase 2: Scaffold Project

```bash
mkdir -p /tmp/{name}-portfolio && cd $_
git init
# Create package.json, tsconfig.json, next.config.js
# Create app/ layout.tsx, page.tsx, globals.css
# Create public/ with favicon, robots.txt, sitemap.xml
```

**Always include:**
- `.gitignore` with `node_modules/`, `.next/`, `out/`, `*.tsbuildinfo`, `next-env.d.ts`
- `package.json` with `output: 'export'` in next.config.js for static hosting
- Semantic HTML with proper heading hierarchy
- OpenGraph metadata in layout.tsx

### Phase 3: Theme System

Define colors in globals.css using CSS variables + `@theme` block for Tailwind 4. See `references/mystic-purple-theme.md` for the full reference palette and unification checklist.

```css
/* Named Color Theme */
:root {
  --color-mystic-50: #f0e6ff;
  ...
  --color-mystic-950: #1a001f;
}
@theme {
  --color-mystic-50: #f0e6ff;
  ...
}
```

Key theme elements:
- Dark gradient background (near-black base)
- Glass-morphism cards (`backdrop-filter: blur(12px)`)
- Gradient accent on text headers (`gradient-text` class)
- Glow effects on CTAs
- Custom scrollbar styling
- Smooth scroll

### Phase 4: Page Structure

Single-page layout with sections:

1. **Nav bar** — sticky, transparent/glass, links to section ids
2. **Hero** — name, title, tagline, stats bar, two CTAs (primary + secondary)
3. **Projects** — card grid (2-3 columns), each with:
   - Color gradient mockup banner (decorative, representing dashboard/app)
   - Title, subtitle, description
   - Tag chips
   - Metric indicators
4. **Skills** — two-column skill bars with percent fill + animated glow
5. **Experience** — timeline layout with bullet points per role
6. **Contact** — centered card with contact channels
7. **Footer** — copyright line

### Phase 5: The "Mystic Purple" Reference

The canonical dark purple portfolio theme uses:
- bg: #0a0012 → #37033e → #1a0025 → #0a0012 gradient
- primary: #8e1aff (mystic-400) and #6a1b7a (mystic-600)
- accent: #d9b3ff (mystic-100) for gradients
- glass cards: rgba(30, 15, 60, 0.6) with blur
- purple glow box-shadows

This is the reference palette. For other themes, replace the color values but keep the same structure.

### Phase 6: Git & Deploy

```bash
cd /tmp/{name}-portfolio
git add -A
git commit -m "Initial commit: {Name} portfolio with {theme} theme"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/{name}-portfolio.git
git push -u origin main
```

**Warning:** If `npm install` ran before .gitignore was created, `git filter-branch` is needed to purge node_modules from git history before push.

### Phase 7: Deploy to Live (Docker Swarm + Traefik)

After git push, deploy as static nginx service on the VPS:

1. **Build export:**
   ```bash
   cd /tmp/{name}-portfolio
   npm install && npx next build
   ```
2. **Create Dockerfile:**
   ```dockerfile
   FROM nginx:alpine
   COPY out /usr/share/nginx/html
   EXPOSE 80
   ```
3. **Build image:**
   ```bash
   docker build -t {name}-portfolio:latest /tmp/{name}-portfolio
   ```
4. **Deploy to Swarm:**
   ```bash
   docker stack deploy -c - {name}-portfolio << 'STACK'
   version: '3.8'
   services:
     web:
       image: {name}-portfolio:latest
       deploy:
         labels:
           - "traefik.enable=true"
           - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.rule=Host(`{subdomain}.paragu-ai.com`)"
           - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.entrypoints=websecure"
           - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
           - "traefik.http.services.{name}-portfolio.loadbalancer.server.port=80"
           - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
       networks:
         - agent-net
   networks:
     agent-net:
       external: true
   STACK
   ```
5. **Verify:** `curl -sI https://{subdomain}.paragu-ai.com` — expect 200

**DNS note:** The domain (`*.paragu-ai.com`) uses Cloudflare proxied DNS. Traefik's HTTP-01 challenge works through the proxy because CF forwards port 80 to the origin. No DNS change needed — just the subdomain must exist on the *record (user adds A record for the VPS IP in CF dashboard).

## Pitfalls

- **Don't commit node_modules or .next** — add .gitignore BEFORE first `git add`, or use `git filter-branch --index-filter 'git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch -r node_modules .next out'` to clean
- **Build artifacts (out/)** should be excluded from git — GitHub Pages deploys via a CI action, not from the repo itself
- **Placeholder content** is fine for the scaffold but note what the user needs to fill: real screenshots, profile photo, actual contact info
- **No fake testimonials** — use "Verified Client" or exclude entirely
- **All color references must be UNIFIED** — don't mix purple/violet/indigo/fuchsia/blue in one theme. Replace ALL occurrences: tailwind classes (`from-purple-...`), CSS `rgba()`, inline style color values, and scrollbar colors. A single missed `rgba(139,92,246,0.4)` will break the unified look. Use `grep -n` to check no old color names remain.
- **Static export only** (`output: 'export'`) — ensures no server dependency. Good for GitHub Pages / Netlify / Cloudflare Pages

## Verification

1. `npm install` succeeds
2. `npx next build` produces clean output with no warnings
3. `git ls-files` shows only source files (no node_modules/.next/out/)
4. Open `out/index.html` — nav links work, sections load, colors match expected theme

{"success": true, "name": "professional-portfolio", "file": "references/mystic-purple-theme.md", "content": "# Mystic Purple Theme Reference

A dark, ethereal, cosmic purple palette for portfolio sites. Deep near-black background with vibrant purple accents.

## Color Palette

| Token       | Hex       | Usage                   |
|-------------|-----------|-------------------------|
| mystic-50   | #f0e6ff   | Lightest accent         |
| mystic-100  | #d9b3ff   | Gradient text light     |
| mystic-200  | #c080ff   | Secondary accent        |
| mystic-300  | #a64dff   | Mid accent              |
| mystic-400  | #8e1aff   | Primary accent (vibrant)|
| mystic-500  | #7b2d8e   | Dark accent             |
| mystic-600  | #6a1b7a   | Card bg, nav bg         |
| mystic-700  | #590f66   | Borders                 |
| mystic-800  | #480852   | Dark cards              |
| mystic-900  | #37033e   | Darkest cards           |
| mystic-950  | #1a001f   | Near-black              |

## Background Gradient

```css
background: linear-gradient(135deg, #0a0012 0%, #1a0025 25%, #37033e 50%, #1a0025 75%, #0a0012 100%);
```

## Glass Card

```css
background: rgba(30, 15, 60, 0.6);
backdrop-filter: blur(12px);
border: 1px solid rgba(142, 26, 255, 0.2);
border-radius: 16px;
```

## Gradient Text

```css
background: linear-gradient(135deg, #d9b3ff, #8e1aff, #c080ff);
-webkit-background-clip: text;
-webkit-text-fill-color: transparent;
```

## Glow (CTA buttons)

```css
box-shadow: 0 0 30px rgba(142, 26, 255, 0.3), 0 0 60px rgba(142, 26, 255, 0.1);
```

## Unification Checklist

After applying the palette, verify NO old colors remain:

```bash
# Check for any non-mystic tailwind purple colors
grep -n 'from-purple\|from-violet\|from-fuchsia\|from-indigo\|from-pink' app/page.tsx

# Check for old rgba references
grep -n 'rgba(139' app/globals.css app/page.tsx

# Check all color references in globals
grep -n '#' app/globals.css | grep -v '#0a\|#1a\|#37\|#8e\|#6a\|#d9\|#c0\|#a6\|#7b\|#59\|#48\|#1a0'
```

This skill is well-structured and covers the build process. But this session revealed several improvements worth adding. The skill mentions "roast"/audit process in passing but doesn't embed it as a step. Also, the deploy section mentions the Swarm pattern but doesn't cover `docker service update` for hot-reload, and the "ugly" feedback was about visual quality — the skill should include a pre-deploy visual quality gate.

Before any deploy, run a browser-based audit to catch layout issues:

1. **Navigate to the live/dev site** and check:
   - Card heights aren't blown out by unstretched child elements (check `.offsetHeight` of cards in browser console)
   - Hero section fills at least 60% of viewport
   - No horizontal overflow
   - Section spacing is balanced (no 5000px+ slabs of content)

2. **Check for 0-image syndrome** — a portfolio with zero real images looks unfinished. Flag this to the user.

3. **Verify the page isn't absurdly long** — total scroll should be under ~12 viewport screens for a single-page portfolio

4. **Common layout killers to catch:**
   - `dash-frame` or mockup containers without explicit `height` — they collapse to ~0px or stretch to 1278px depending on container context
   - Cards with too much text per project — keep descriptions to 1-2 lines with `line-clamp-2`
   - Hero section too small — use `min-h-screen` instead of a fixed height
   - No images at all (add decorative icons/SVGs as placeholder)
   - System fonts with no character — at minimum use a custom `system-ui` stack. For premium feel, load Google Fonts (Inter, Plus Jakarta Sans) via preload link in layout.tsx

## Phase 6: Hot-Reload Updates (fix after deploy)

When user says it "looks ugly" and you've already deployed:

1. Fix CSS and TSX locally in `/tmp/{name}-portfolio`
2. Rebuild: `cd /tmp/{name}-portfolio && npx next build`
3. Rebuild Docker: `docker build -t {name}-portfolio:latest .`
4. Hot-swap: `docker service update --image {name}-portfolio:latest {name}-portfolio_web`
5. Verify immediate: `docker run --rm --network agent-net nginx:alpine curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://{name}-portfolio_web/`
6. Commit and push the fix to git

**Performance note:** `docker service update` with `--image` reuses the same tag (`latest`) but Swarm detects the new digest and rolls out the update. If the digest doesn't change (same image layers), add a no-op layer: `RUN echo $(date +%s) > /build-id` in Dockerfile.

## Phase 7: Deploy to Live (Docker Swarm + Traefik)

   ```
3. **Build image:**

   ```
4. **Deploy to Swarm:**

   version: '3.8'

           - "traefik.enable=true"
           - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.rule=Host(`{subdomain}.paragu-ai.com`)"
           - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.entrypoints=websecure"
           - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
           - "traefik.http.services.{name}-portfolio.loadbalancer.server.port=80"
           - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
       networks:
         - agent-net
   networks:
     agent-net:
       external: true
   STACK
   ```
5. **Verify:** `curl -sI https://{subdomain}.paragu-ai.com` — expect 200

**DNS note:** The domain (`*.paragu-ai.com`) uses Cloudflare proxied DNS. Traefik's HTTP-01 challenge works through the proxy because CF forwards port 80 to the origin. No DNS change needed — just the subdomain must exist on the * record (user adds A record for the VPS IP in CF dashboard)."}

{"action": "write_file", "file_path": "references/visual-audit-checklist.md", "name": "professional-portfolio", "content": "# Visual Audit Checklist — Portfolio Sites

Run these checks before calling a site "done"/"deployed". Use browser console or curl to measure.

## Layout

- [ ] Cards have proper height — no 1400px cards from unstretched children
- [ ] `dash-frame` or mockup elements have explicit `height` + `!important` if needed
- [ ] No horizontal overflow (`document.documentElement.scrollWidth <= window.innerWidth`)
- [ ] Total scroll < 12 viewport screens for single-page
- [ ] Hero is >60% of viewport height (`min-h-screen` or equivalent)
- [ ] Font loaded (check `document.querySelector('link[href*="font"]')` exists or body font is intentional)

## Content

- [ ] At least decorative icons in project cards (zero images looks unfinished)
- [ ] Descriptions use `line-clamp-2` for card height consistency
- [ ] No placeholder text visible ("Lorem ipsum", empty "[]", uninformative "Click here")
- [ ] Skill bars animate or have visual fill indicator
- [ ] Timeline/experience has proper visual hierarchy (active role bold, past role dimmed)

## Theme

- [ ] All colors unified — no `purple`, `violet`, `indigo`, `fuchsia`, `pink` tailwind classes mixed in
- [ ] All `rgba()` references use the primary accent color, not an old palette
- [ ] Scrollbar is styled (matches theme)
- [ ] Links/CTAs have hover states
- [ ] Contact section has interactive elements (hover, cursor)

## Technical

- [ ] `npm run build` passes clean (no warnings)
- [ ] `git ls-files` shows only source (no node_modules/.next/out)
- [ ] Static export works (check `out/index.html` directly)
- [ ] Docker build succeds
- [ ] Swarm service shows `1/1` replicas
- [ ] `curl https://domain.com` returns 200
- [ ] SSL cert valid (no 526 Cloudflare error)

## Common Fixes

| Problem | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| Card 1400px tall | Add `height: 112px !important` to `.dash-frame` |
| No images look bad | Use SVG bar chart icons as placeholder OR add library like react-icons |
| Page too long | Reduce project descriptions to 1 line, use smaller card padding |
| Skill bars static | Add `skill-bar-inner` animation: `@keyframes skillFill { from { width: 0 } to { width: var(--skill-width) } }` |
| Hero flat | Add animated gradient orbs: 3 `div`s with `blur-3xl`, different sizes, `animation: orbFloat`
| Font boring | Preload Google Fonts (Inter, Plus Jakarta Sans) in layout.tsx |
| No hover states | Add `group-hover:` on card elements, `transition-all` on buttons, `hover:border-color` |
"}
